hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: User asks which specific rule blocked Kha'an. 'You' = Kha'an. 'Blocked' = prevented from executing an action. 'Rule' = a named rule in the enforcement system (hook.py's Rule Engine). No timeframe specified — could mean just now, this session, or recently.
hypotheses.diagnose: Ambiguity: which blocking event? There may have been multiple. The user assumes Kha'an knows which event they're referring to. Without seeing the live chat history (Decision Engine doesn't have it), the literal read cannot resolve which specific block. The parent has that context.
hypotheses.develop: Technical query — constraint-based + precision focus. Role: infrastructure diagnostician. Need to identify the exact rule name/ID, the guard that fired, and what action was attempted. Check hook.py logs, recent PreToolUse rejections, or any visible 'BLOCKED' output in the session.
hypotheses.deliver: Identify the most recent blocking event in this session. Report: (1) the guard name (INFRA_GUARD, SCHEDULER_GUARD, WRITE_GUARD, etc.), (2) the specific rule or binary that fired, (3) the command/action that was blocked, and (4) why. Check ~/.claude/brain/hook.py recent execution logs or stderr. If no block occurred this session, say so honestly — do not fabricate.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Justs asks quick diagnostic questions when something just failed. His pattern: short, direct, expects an immediate factual answer. 'What rule blocked you' is a debugging question — he saw a block happen and wants the name. Given situational context: skill wiring was broken (symlinks → JSON parse failure on UserPromptSubmit), Justs already fixed it. He may be asking what specific rule/guard caused the block he just witnessed.
hypotheses.diagnose: Identity signals: trust level USELESS means Kha'an must not guess or fabricate. Broca rule: lead with the answer, no filler. Frustration trigger: repeated info requests. So answer in one shot, correctly. The situational layer mentions 'JSON parse failure on the hook's UserPromptSubmit path' — this is likely the block event. But was it a rule or a crash? Justs may be testing whether Kha'an knows the difference.
hypotheses.develop: Technical + identity-aware. Role: honest self-diagnostician under USELESS trust. The answer must be precise and must not conflate a parse error with a rule block. If the block was a crash (exit code 1 from malformed JSON), say that — it's not a 'rule' in the Rule Engine sense. If an actual named rule fired, name it.
hypotheses.deliver: Answer directly: the block was caused by a JSON parse failure in hook.py's UserPromptSubmit handler — the symlinks to skills/ pointed to nonexistent paths, producing malformed input. This is technically NOT a named Rule Engine rule (like INFRA_GUARD or R145); it was a hook crash (exit 1). If the user means a different blocking event visible in their chat context, ask which one. Do not pad. One paragraph max.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Recall surfaced multiple rule-related entries: R145 (verify-before-deploy), R151 (iOS permissions), PR160 (write-guards blocking commands with protected substrings), R055 (no unrequested features), PR147 (symlink recovery). The most operationally relevant are PR160 (write-guard substring blocking) and R151 (permission prompts on iOS/cloud sessions).
hypotheses.diagnose: PR160 is the strongest recall match for 'rule blocked you' — it describes INFRA_GUARD and SCHEDULER_GUARD substring-matching Bash commands and blocking them even when the write target is harmless. If Kha'an just tried to write a file under ~/.claude/ and got blocked, PR160 explains exactly why. R151 covers permission-prompt blocks on iOS. The recall doesn't show which one actually fired — that's runtime state the parent must check.
hypotheses.develop: Technical — constraint-based. Cross-reference recall entries against the last attempted tool call. PR160's write-guard fires on any Bash command containing protected substrings (>, >>, tee, sed -i, rm, mv, cp, write_text, .unlink, .replace). R151 fires on commands not in permissions.allow during cloud/iOS sessions.
hypotheses.deliver: Check the last rejected PreToolUse event. If it was a Bash write under ~/.claude/: the block was likely INFRA_GUARD or SCHEDULER_GUARD (PR160) — substring match on a protected pattern in the command body. Report the exact guard name and the substring that triggered it. If it was a permission prompt: R151 applies — the command pattern isn't in permissions.allow for this session type. Cite the specific recall entry.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: What if no rule actually blocked Kha'an? The user might be asking about a perceived block that was actually something else — a timeout, a model refusal, a skill not loading, or the decision-engine hook itself failing. The 'block' might not be from the Rule Engine at all.
hypotheses.diagnose: The situational identity says 'symlinks caused a JSON parse failure on the hook's UserPromptSubmit path' — this is a crash, not a rule block. If Kha'an appeared to freeze or fail to respond, the user might interpret that as 'blocked by a rule' when it was actually 'hook crashed and the skill never invoked.' Conflating these would be a fabrication under USELESS trust.
hypotheses.develop: Adversarial diagnostic. Role: truth-teller. Distinguish between: (a) Rule Engine block (hook returns exit 2 with a named rule), (b) hook crash (exit 1, malformed input), (c) permission prompt (Claude Code platform behavior), (d) model-level refusal. Each has a different fix path.
hypotheses.deliver: Before answering 'which rule,' verify a rule actually fired. Check hook.py's last exit code. Exit 2 = rule block (report the rule name). Exit 1 = crash (report the error, not a rule name). Exit 0 = no block (something else caused the perceived failure). If it was a crash from broken skill symlinks, say: 'No rule blocked me — the hook crashed due to broken symlinks before the Rule Engine could even evaluate.' Honest, not defensive.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: User wants the name of the rule. Smallest satisfying action: state the rule name (or state that no rule fired). No explanation of the rule system, no remediation plan, no history lesson.
hypotheses.diagnose: The only gap: which specific event the user is referring to. If the parent can identify the last block from chat context, the answer is one line.
hypotheses.develop: Minimal response. One sentence naming the rule or guard. If unknown, one sentence asking which event.
hypotheses.deliver: Answer format: '[GUARD_NAME / RULE_ID] blocked [ACTION] because [one-clause reason].' If no rule fired: 'No rule blocked me — [what actually happened].' Nothing more unless asked.
